coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated graphene as an enhanced sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II)
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Zhao, Shujie
Li, Meina
Li, Weiwei
Zhao, Yingying
Qi, Jurui
Cui, Xiaoqiang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple and efficient electrochemical sensing platform was designed based on the hybrid of two carbon nanomaterials for the sensitive and selective detection of trace Cu(II) by using differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) analysis. The GQDs/graphene/GCE exhibited enhanced electrochemical responses because of a fast electron transfer from graphene and a high affinity to Cu(II) from graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Under the optimal conditions, a good linear response in the range of 0.015–8.775μM with correlation coefficient of 0.9995 and a detection limit of 1.34nM (S/N=3) were obtained. The developed method was further validated and successfully applied to the detection of Cu(II) in real water samples. The simple preparation and efficient selectivity of the proposed modified electrode show the promising potential for applications in sensing of Cu(II).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical senor
Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Copper(II)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterizations of the modified electrode
The mechanism of the electrochemical reaction between Cu(II) and GQDs
The optimization of the experimental conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images, (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) FL spectra (emission spectrum of the GQDs at 448nm excitation wavelength and excitation spectrum of GQDs at 525nm emission wavelength), and (D) Emission spectra with excitation of different wavelengths of the GQDs.

(A) The CV curves measured in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3− containing 0.1M KCl with scan rate of 50mVs−1. (Bare GCE (curve a), GQDs/GCE (curve b), graphene/GCE (curve c), and GQDs/graphene/GCE (curve d).) (B) The typical Nyquist plots in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1M KCl with frequency varied from 100kHz to 0.1Hz at open circuit potential. Inset was the equivalent circuit. R
s : bulk resistance, R ct: charge transfer resistance, C
dl : constant phase element, Z
w : Warburg. (C) The DPASV responses for the determination of 2.0μM Cu(II) in Na2HPO4-C6H8O7 buffer solutions obtained at bare GCE (curve a), GQDs/GCE (curve b), graphene/GCE (curve c), and GQDs/graphene/GCE (curve d). DPASV conditions: amplitude of 100mV, pulse width of 10ms, and potential step of 5mV.

(A) DPASV responses obtained at the GQDs/graphene/GCE under the optimized variables of pH values of (a) 2.0, (b) 3.0, (c) 4.0, (d) 5.0, (e) 6.0, and (f) 7.0. (B) Scheme 2. Schematic illustration for the electrochemical reaction between Cu(II) and GQDs.

Effect of the (A) anodic stripping measurements, (B) amount of modifiers, (C) type of supporting electrolytes, (D) pH value of supporting electrolytes, (E) deposition time, and (F) deposition potential on the DPASV response for GQDs/graphene/GCE.

(A) The DPASV responses for GQDs/graphene/GCE toward different concentrations of Cu(II). Inset: the calibration curves of the DPASV peak currents vs. concentrations of Cu(II) in the range of 0.015μM–8.775μM. The DPASV peak current ratios (I
a /I
o ) in the presence of different potential interfering inorganic metallic ions (B) and coexisting organic substances (C) with respect to copper ions, where I
a and I
o were the stripping peak current of Cu(II) with the addition and absence of interfering metal ions, respectively. (D) The evaluation of the interfering effect of 1.6μM Pb(II) and Cd(II) on the DPASV responses. (E) The electrode reproducibility of 12 consecutive current measurements for the same electrode. (F) The electrode stability over one month of 1.6μM Cu(II) under the optimal conditions.

Schematic illustration for the whole experimental process: (A) the synthesis of GQDs by pyrolyzing CA, (B) the fabrication of the graphene/GQDs modified electrode, and (C) the application for the detection of Cu(II).
Comparison of some characteristics of sensing platforms for the determination of Cu(II).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NH2-rGO: amino-reduced graphene oxide; β-CD: β-cyclodextrin; Gr: graphene; MWCNT: multiwall carbonnanotube; FMC: CdTe nanocrystals grown in situ in thiol-modified MCM-41 mesoporous silica; L-cys: L-cysteine; AuNPs: gold nanoparticles; CdS: CdS nanospheres.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Cu(II) in real drinking water samples. (Degree of confidence 95%, n=3.)
Recoveries of Cu(II) in real drinking water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 20μgL−1 Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, K+, and Na+.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated graphene as an enhanced sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Automotive Simulation and Control, Department of Materials Science, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Automotive Simulation and Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple and efficient electrochemical sensing platform was designed based on the hybrid of two carbon nanomaterials for the sensitive and selective detection of trace Cu(II) by using differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) analysis. The GQDs/graphene/GCE exhibited enhanced electrochemical responses because of a fast electron transfer from graphene and a high affinity to Cu(II) from graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Under the optimal conditions, a good linear response in the range of 0.015–8.775μM with correlation coefficient of 0.9995 and a detection limit of 1.34nM (S/N=3) were obtained. The developed method was further validated and successfully applied to the detection of Cu(II) in real water samples. The simple preparation and efficient selectivity of the proposed modified electrode show the promising potential for applications in sensing of Cu(II).

ce:section.ce:para: Copper(II), as the third most abundant transition metal in the human body, plays vital roles in all living organisms [1]. The overload of Cu(II) may result in irreparable ecological environmental contamination [2] and induce potential risks on human health. It is essential to develop high sensitive and selective methods for monitoring and measuring of the trace Cu(II). Up to now, various analytical techniques have been reported for the detection of Cu(II) including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [3], fluorescence spectrometry [4], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) [5], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [6], etc. Compared to other counterparts, electroanalytical techniques [7] show the advantages of simple operation, time-efficient determination, and remarkable sensitivity as a powerful tool for the analysis of heavy metal ions [8,9].

The chemically modified electrode (CME) plays a key role for an electrochemical sensing system, in which the modified layer is supposed to specifically capture the targets [10]. Several modified electrodes have been reported for the electrochemical detection of Cu(II) directly via stripping voltammetry. The sensing layer of the corresponding modified materials involved noble metal nanoparticles [11,12], metal oxides, organic molecules [13,14] and carbon-based materials [15]. Among the aforementioned candidates, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted much attention because of the excellent electric properties, low price, and relative simple synthesis. In particular, graphene, a kind of significant two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, possesses extraordinarily electrochemical properties owing to its large specific surface area, high electron transfer rate, and relative chemical inertness in electrolyte solutions [16,17]. Many researches have been explored on the applications of graphene for the in-situ measurement of metal ions in water [18]. Recently, Lu's group [19] reported an enhanced ultrasensitive Cu(II) sensing platform by using the graphene–SnS nanocomposites. Huang's group [14] developed a highly sensitive and selective Cu(II) sensor using amino-reduced graphene oxide and β-cyclodextrin. Although these studies have demonstrated that graphene is an ideal nanomaterial in heavy metal detection, there are still plenty of rooms for developing novel graphene-based materials for the high performancing Cu(II) sensor.

Quantum dots (QDs) can serve as a kind of ideal heavy metal ion sensing elements because of their unique optical properties, versatile surface chemical and ligand binding ability [20]. Among which, the GQDs, as the incredibly small pieces of graphene [21], also present some of unusual optoelectronic properties of graphene due to their quantum confinement and edge effects [22]. These significant properties and its non-toxicity provide promising applications in the fabrication of heavy metal sensors. Previous studies utilizing GQDs on Cu(II) sensors are mainly focused on their fluorescence intensity changes. For instance, Wang's group [23] developed a fluorescent sensing platform based on GQDs for highly detection of Cu(II). However, seldom reports are available on GQDs-based composites utilizing their edge and synergistic effects as modified electrodes for the detection of Cu(II). In this work, we constructed a new and sensitive platform composing of graphene and GQDs for the detection of trace Cu(II). Specifically, graphene was chosen to accelerate charge-transport, and the residual oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs (mainly COOH) were utilized to bind Cu(II) in the meantime. Moreover, due to the electrostatic attraction, ion-exchange and hydrophilia between the two candidates, the system provided high ratios of active recognizing sites toward Cu(II).

Citric acid (CA), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, nitric acid and metal salts were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Graphene was purchased from Nanjing JC Nano. Tech. Co. Ltd., China. All other reagents were commercially available as analytical reagent grade without further purification. The working stock of 1.000mg/L Cu2+ solution was prepared referred to the GB/T 14353-93 by dissolving 0.100g of pure copper wire in 20mL of HNO3 solution (HNO3:H2O=1:1, v/v) and diluting to 100mL. Other metal ions were prepared by dissolving metal salts in the minimum required amount of nitric acid and were then appropriately diluted prior to experiments. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (≥18.2MΩ·cm) purified by a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, USA). Na2HPO4-C6H8O7 buffer solutions with various pH values from 2.0 to 7.0 were prepared by mixing stock solutions of 0.10M C6H8O7·H2O with 0.20M Na2HPO4·2H2O.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a computer-controlled CHI650C electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) with a standard three-electrode system. A bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (diameter of 3mm, CHI104) or modified GCE served as the working electrode. A platinum wire was used as counter-electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE, Hg/HgCl2 in saturated KCl) was used as the reference electrode. All electrochemical experiments were carried out in an airtight electrochemical cell at room temperature.

The morphological characterization of GQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN, 200kV, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FTIR spectrophotometer (8400S, Shimadzu, Japan). The zeta potential of GQDs was measured by a dynamic light-scattering (DLS) instrument (Autosizer 4700, Malvern, UK). All fluorescence (FL) spectra were determined by using a FL spectrophotometer (RF 5301, Shimadzu, Japan). An atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) system (ICE-3500, Thermo Fisher) was used for measuring the individual concentrations of the various metal ions in the solution.

GQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of the CA according to previous literature described in supporting information [24]. Before the modification of GCE, bare GCE was carefully polished to get a mirror-like surface with emery paper and chamois leather containing 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05μm alumina slurry, and then sonicated in acetone, ethanol, and ultrapure water, subsequently. After polishing, the electrode was rinsed with ultrapure water and transferred to the electrochemical cell for the activation by cycling 10 times in 0.50M H2SO4 between −0.20V and 0.60V with scan rate of 50mVs−1 until a stable profile was obtained. Then it was cleaned with ultrapure water and ethanol, and dried under a nitrogen flow.

The graphene/GQDs modified electrode was prepared by the following procedures. A certain amount of graphene was added into DMF and then sonicated for 2h to give a homogeneous dispersion (0.50mgmL−1). 5μL of as-prepared suspension was dropped onto a freshly polished GCE and dried in air at room temperature. A stock solution of 0.50mgmL−1 GQDs were prepared by dispersing 2.5mg GQDs in 5mL of ultrapure water using an ultrasonic bath until a homogeneous suspension were obtained. Then 5μL of the dispersion of GQDs was pipetted onto the surface of graphene layer and dried at ambient condition.

The solution was bubbled with high-purity nitrogen to deoxygenate for at least 10min prior to each experiment and a continuous flow of nitrogen was maintained above the solution. Differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) experiments for the electrochemical analysis of Cu(II) were carried out in a cell containing 40mL of supporting electrolyte (C6H8O7-Na2HPO4, pH=3.0) under optimized conditions as follows.

A negative potential of −0.70V was applied to the modified electrode for the electrochemically deposition of 180s with efficiently stirring (above 200rpm) to preconcentration and reduce Cu(II) into elemental metallic Cu(0). After a period of 20s rest, the DPASV measurement was performed in the potential range from −0.30V to +0.15V. The modified electrode was renewed in a fresh supporting electrolyte for 60s at +0.30V to remove the previous residual Cu(0). It was further checked by the DPASV measurement to ensure no interference to the next determination within the potential range. The schematic illustration of the modified process on GCE and its application for the detection of Cu(II) were shown in Scheme 1 .

TEM images in Fig. 1A showed that the GQDs were of dot-like structure with good monodispersity and the diameter of approximately 5nm. FTIR was used to characterize structural insight of the obtained GQDs (Fig. 1B). A broad peak at 3431cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of OH, a small peak at 1689cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of CO from carboxyl groups [24]. The two peaks located at 1556 and 1390cm−1 were attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate radical groups, respectively, suggesting that the main existing forms of carboxyl group was COO−, which is consistent with the result of zeta potentials of GQDs at −16.5mV. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1C–D were used to further study the optical properties that closely related to the dot size and carbonization degree of GQDs [24]. On the excitation of 448nm, the emission spectrum showed a strong peak at 525nm. During the changing of the excitation wavelength from 380 to 460nm, the emission wavelength was nearly excitation-independent indicating that both the size and the surface state of the GQDs were uniform [25,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterizations of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical behaviors and electrochemical reaction kinetics on the interface of electrode/solution were investigated by cyclic voltammogram (CV). CV responses of bare GCE, graphene/GCE, GQDs/GCE, and GQDs/graphene/GCE in 5mM of [Fe(CN)6]3− containing 0.1M KCl were shown in Fig. 2A at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The CV of a bare GCE showed a quasi-reversible redox reaction with the peak-to-peak separation (ΔE P) of 111mV and the ratio of anodic and cathodic peak currents was about 1:1 (curve a). The decreased peak currents after the modification of GQDs were ascribed to the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs (mainly COO−) and the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− couple (curve b) [27]. On the contrary, the CV peak currents increased obviously when graphene was modified to GCE, indicating that graphene significantly enhanced the electron transport between the electrochemical probe and electrode surface. Furthermore, the GQDs/graphene/GCE provided a better electrochemical behavior than bare GCE and GQDs/GCE. The real electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) and the corresponding standard electrochemical rate constant (ҟϴ) indicated that the introduction of graphene increased the effective electrode surface and electrical conductivity at the electrode/solution interface as shown in Fig. S1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) also gave detailed information about the change of the interface properties during the modification process as shown in Fig. 2B. GQDs/GCE showed a remarkable increase of the semicircle diameter in comparison with bare GCE, suggesting that GQDs blocked the electron transfer efficiency due to the electrostatic repulsion. In contrast, after the electrode was modified with graphene, a significant decrease in R ct was observed and the semicircle portion displayed an almost straight line (curve d). This much smaller R ct than both bare GCE and GQDs/GCE was observed after the confirmation of the accelerating effects on the electron transfer by introducing graphene.

The electrochemical behavior of GQDs/graphene/GCE was studied by DPASV in 2.0μM Cu(II) solution as shown in Fig. 2C. GQDs/GCE showed a larger DPASV peak current than that of the bare GCE and graphene/GCE at −0.070V versus SCE, indicating the specific response to Cu(II) from the GQDs. A more remarkable stripping signal was obtained on the GQDs/graphene/GCE, which was attributed to the excellent conductivity of graphene and the synergy effect between graphene and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the electrochemical reaction between Cu(II) and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The above results showed that the GQDs/graphene/GCE was successfully used for Cu(II) detection in aqueous solutions. To validate the electrochemical reaction between Cu(II) and GQDs, the possible mechanism for this electrochemical process was investigated. Fig. 3A showed that the anodic stripping peak potential (E pa) negatively shifted with the increase of pH values, which indicated that the proton transfer was involved in the process of this electrochemical reaction [28]. The number of electrons involved in the reaction was calculated through Eq. (1) [29]:
(1)
E =
E θ
-
0.059 υ
/ n
pH
where υ is the number of protons, n is the number of electrons involved in the complete reaction. The regression equation between the E pa and pH was E pa(V)=−0.0499 pH+0.0769 (R2 =0.9923) (Fig. 3a). The slop value of 49.9mVpH−1 was closed to the theoretical Nernstian value of 59mVpH−1, which was proved that the whole electrochemical process transferred the equal number of electrons and protons in accordance with Nernst equation. As a consequence, the electron transfer number (n) of Cu(II) was 2, the number of protons (υ) was calculated through Eq. (1) to be 1.69 (≈2). Therefore, a hypothetical mechanism was proposed as the followings. In general, the electron accepting ability of Cu(II) was stronger than that of H+ in the solution. The concentration of Cu(II) on the electrode surface was higher than the bulk solution during the progress of preconcentration. Therefore, the hydrogen ions combined with carboxylate ions on the GQDs showed a tendency to experience the ion-exchange process with Cu(II) in the solution. Taking the above factors into consideration, the detailed reaction mechanism was presented below (Fig. 3B Scheme 2):
Cu
2 +
aq
+
GQDs‐2COOH → GQDs‐2COO -
‐
Cu
2 +
+ 2
H +
ion‐exchange
GQDs‐ 2
COO −
‐
Cu
2 +
+ 2
e −
+ 2
H +
→
Cu
0
GCE
surface
+ GQDs‐2COOH
reduction
Cu
0
GCE
surface
→
Cu
2 +
aq
+ 2
e −
oxidation

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of the experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions were carefully optimized to get the maximum sensitivity for trace Cu(II) detection with GQDs/graphene/GCE. The anodic stripping measurements using the CV, linear sweep anodic stripping voltammetry (LSASV), square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV), and DPASV in 1.6μM Cu(II) were evaluated as shown in Fig. 4A. DPASV exhibited the highest response with well-defined anodic peak. Although the SWASV was normally a more sensitive technique, the high scan rates minimized the capacitive current to noise [30], which resulted in a relative low repeatability, reproductivity, and a narrow linear range in this case and was not beneficial to the detection in real drinking water samples [31]. Therefore, DPASV was chosen for further experiments. The weight ratios of GQDs to graphene were optimized by fixing the mass concentration of the graphene at 0.50mgmL−1 and then changing the mass concentration of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4B, the current response toward Cu(II) increased with increasing the weight ratio of GQDs to graphene. The slope of this plot tended to be decreased at the weight ratio of 1.0 because of the relative poor electron transfer efficiency. Therefore, we chose 1.0 as the optimized weight ratio of GQDs to graphene.

The supporting electrolyte will affect the complexation between carboxyl group and Cu(II). Fig. 4C showed the DPASV responses of 1.2μM Cu(II) in the buffer solutions of C6H8O7-Na2HPO4, NaAc-HAc, C8H6O4-HCl, and C6H8O7-Na3C6H5O7 at pH of 3.0. The types of buffers were limited in the acidic range to prevent the hydrolysis of Cu(II). The different electrochemical responses of Cu(II) and stripping potentials in different electrolytes were possibly attributed to various complexation and surface activity of the Cu(0) deposits during the accumulation step in different electrolyte solutions [32]. The results showed that the highest stripping signal and well-presented peak shape were obtained in C6H8O7-Na2HPO4 solution. Therefore, it was chosen as supporting electrolyte for the further work. The pH value of the supporting electrolyte has profound effect on the voltammetric behavior of metal ion. As shown in Fig. 4D, a highest stripping peak current of Cu(II) was observed at pH3.0. This was probably because that lower pH than 3.0 would result in the protonation of oxygen-containing hydrophilic groups on GQDs, and the higher pH would result in the hydrolysis of Cu(II) [33].

The influence of deposition time on the peak current was investigated in 1.2μM Cu(II). As shown in Fig. 4E, the stripping peak currents reached to a plateau at 180s, indicating a surface saturation and equilibrium attainment between the adsorption/complex formation and desorption/complex dissociation [34]. Thus, 180s was employed as the deposition time in all subsequent experiments. The effects of the deposition potential were investigated as shown in Fig. 4F. A highest stripping peak current was obtained at −0.70V. This optimization of the potential might be ascribed to the evolution of hydrogen that may damage the stability of Cu(0) accumulation on the electrode surface [35]. In addition, the other instrumental parameters including pulse width, pulse amplitude, and potential step were also studied to obtain the optimum conditions (Fig. S2a–c).

The analytical performance of GQDs/graphene/GCE for the detection of Cu(II) was validated with respect to the linearity and limit of detection, accuracy and precision, selectivity and interference study, reproducibility and stability studies, robustness and ruggedness according to the ICH guidelines [36].

High sensitive detection of Cu(II) was realized on GQDs/graphene/GCE under the optimized conditions. The stripping peak currents of Cu(II) were positively proportional to its concentration as shown in Fig. 5A. Good correlation with a wide dynamic range of 0.015μM to 8.775μM was obtained. The equation of the calibration curve was I pa =20.314 C +0.6611 (R 2 =0.9995), where I pa (μA) was the anodic stripping peak current and C (μM) was the concentration of Cu(II). The corresponding sensitivity from the slope of the calibration curve was 20.314μAμM−1. The limits of detection (LOD), defined as C LOD =3S
b /b, where S
b was the standard deviation of 6 replicate blank signals and b was the slope of the linear section of calibration curve [37], had been calculated to be 1.34nM, which was much lower than the principal health-based guideline values of 1.3mg/L (20.46μM) for Cu(II) in drinking water set by the US Environmental Protection Agency [38]. Moreover, a detailed comparison among different sensing platforms documented in literatures for the determination of Cu(II) was made in Table 1 .

The accuracy of the graphene/GQDs/GCE sensor was good by comparing the detecting responses with the Graphite Furnace Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (GFAAS) as shown in Fig. S3 and Table 2 . The recovery studies were performed by spiking a known amount of Cu(II) in samples as depicted in Table 3 . The recoveries of Cu(II) ranged from 90.20% to 115.89% with the relative standard deviation (RSD%) values of <3%. Moreover, the precision was determined for three concentrations of Cu(II) solutions as shown in Table S1. The above results revealed that this method possessed good accuracy and high precision for the detection of Cu(II).

To evaluate the possible interferences that may be present in the water samples, several potential interfering inorganic metallic ions and coexisting organic substances were studied under the optimized method (Fig. 5B and C). The DPASV peak current ratios with respect to Cu(II) only slightly changed from 0.911 to 1.062 within 15 times of each interfering inorganic metallic ions of Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, K+, Na+ and 100 times of each interfering organic substances of CHCl3, CH2Cl2, CHBr3, C6H5Cl, and C6Cl5OH. The tolerance limits of the interferences error was <±5% as shown in Table S2. Most metallic ions possessed a weak decrease of the peak current ascribed to the occupying of the active sites from the co-deposition [39]. The peak current increased from Hg2+ could be attributed to the formation of Cu-Hg intermetallic compounds [40]. The slight decrease of the responses from the organic interferences exhibited that GQDs had low adsorption affinity to the organic compounds. In conclusion, it was affirmed that no significant interference was observed. Interestingly, we found that the GQDs/graphene/GCE also revealed obvious different oxidation peaks at −0.53V from Pb(II) and at −0.82V from Cd(II) as shown in Fig. 5D. That meant that Pb(II) and Cd(II) could also be quantitative detected in a certain range (Fig. S4). This appearance of DPASV responses at different potentials not only generated negligible disturbance toward the selective detection of Cu(II), but also provided a potential probability for working on simultaneous detection on several trace heavy metals.

The reproducibility and stability of sensor are also key factors in practical application. The modified electrode was readily renewed in a fresh stirred supporting electrolyte after each stripping experiment. Fig. 5E showed the DPASV responses to 1.6μM Cu(II) for 12 times of repetitive measurements on regenerated modified electrode. We found that the stripping peak currents of sensor exhibited no observable change with the RSD% of <4.20%. The stability tests of the same prepared electrode stored for one month at room temperature showed that 90.97% of original responses to Cu(II) was maintained as shown in Fig. 5F.

The robustness of the proposed method was evaluated by making small changes in the pH value of supporting electrolyte and experimental temperature. The capacity of this method remained unaffected based on the RSD% values of the above parameters summarized in Table S3. The range of RSD% values were 0.50–1.43% (pH value of supporting electrolyte) and 0.16–0.79% (experimental temperature) suggesting the high precision of this method. Method ruggedness was assessed by analysis of the same procedure applied by three different analysts and instruments expressed as RSD% listed in Table S3. The inter-analysts RSD% was in a range of 0.08–0.85% and the range of inter-instruments RSD% was 0.02–0.91%, which revealed that the proposed method was rugged.

In summary, the graphene/GQDs-based electrochemical senor was successfully designed and implemented for the trace Cu(II) detection using DPASV. The combination of the merits of graphene and GQDs enhanced the sensing performances with a low detection limit of 1.34nM in a wide linear range of 0.015μM to 8.775μM for Cu(II). This platform also showed high sensitivity, good selectivity, reproducibility, and stability. The analysis of Cu(II) in various drinking water sample was realized with satisfactory results, indicating that this method provides a promising potential for the heavy metal detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFA0200400), the Natural Science Foundation of China (51571100, 21275064), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Programme of Higher Education (20130061110035), and the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University (2016075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.05.031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper-induced modifications of the trophic relations in riverine algal-bacterial biofilms

A review on detection of heavy metal ions in water – an electrochemical approach
Coordination chemistry in two dimensions: chemically modified electrodes

Development of gold-doped carbon foams as a sensitive electrochemical sensor for simultaneous determination of Pb (II) and Cu (II)
